retrieved_skills.skill_text: Given the green, fertile landscape and architectural cues, the village is likely in central or eastern Ukraine.
Utility poles and street design are consistent with Korean infrastructure standards.
The road is unpaved and appears to be a rural residential street, common in Eastern European countries.
The street is paved with asphalt, and the absence of painted lane lines on a residential street fits many small towns in these regions.
The white road delineators with a single black rectangle are characteristic of those found in Germany and the Czech Republic, specifically in rural areas.
The street architecture shows houses with flat roofs and walled facades, common in Mexican cities.
The lane markings are a single dashed line, which is a style frequently seen in Poland and the Baltic countries.
The utility pole and street lamp style match infrastructure seen in Caribbean countries.
The road is very wide with a simple dashed white center line and solid white shoulder lines, a style common in Russia and nearby post-Soviet countries.
No visible language or street signs, but the lack of Cyrillic with this architecture hints against Russia, Ukraine, or Belarus, narrowing it more toward Poland or perhaps the Czech Republic.
The road is paved but appears to be of middling quality, with a faded white dashed center line and no shoulder markings, suggesting rural or remote areas.
The street layout and the presence of a detached single-family home structure in a tidy residential neighborhood further point toward France.
